evidence: Multiple modules implement the same dependency-injection shape as large function-field bags plus near-identical merge/default plumbing (e.g., StartupRuntime in startuporchestrator, RectifyRuntime in rectify, Runtime*Ops and dockerRuntime in docker/orchestration, workflowRuntime in shipworkflow, importerRuntime in importer).

Several adapters are pure field-copy pass-through layers (e.g., wireguardRuntimeFromDocker and urbitRuntimeFromDocker) and default runtime loaders call through additional wrappers without adding policy.

Cross-module services read/write broad config models directly (SysConfig and embedded UrbitDocker concerns) instead of narrower capability interfaces, increasing dependency surface and reducing abstraction leverage.

Runtime dependency wiring is duplicated across multiple orchestration layers: `defaultRuntimeContainerOps`/`defaultRuntimeUrbit` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, `newDockerRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_docker.go`, and projection builders like `NewDockerTransitionRuntime` in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_core.go` all manually remap overlapping function sets.

System modules combine unrelated responsibilities: `goseg/system/system.go` mixes host metrics, disk/mount scanning, and cron installer behavior; `goseg/system/disk.go` mixes filesystem provisioning, fstab mutation, tmp symlink policy, and SMART health checks.

Config update flow uses two disconnected mutation models: reflection/string-path dispatch in `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` and JSON map re-serialization in `goseg/config/persistence.go`, increasing hidden coupling and runtime-only validation.

Test files repeatedly implement local seam-reset scaffolding (`resetDiskSeams`, `resetBackupsvcDependencies`, token/session override helpers), indicating missing shared harness conventions across modules.
goseg/importer/importer.go:927 unconditionally wraps and returns runtime.runImportedPierWorkflowFn result with fmt.Errorf(...%w...), so nil from the runtime still becomes a non-nil error.
goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go:64-66 and 129-152 log persistence/update failures and continue; Reconcile still returns nil at line 157.

goseg/importer/importer.go mixes bare string errors for auth/session failures (lines 232, 245, 247, 385, 389, 395, 403) while other flows use sentinel/joined errors; goseg/importer/importer_test.go asserts message text fragments.
goseg/system/system.go:208-212 treats any crontab read error as 'not found', but addCron (lines 223-227) distinguishes benign no-crontab from real failures.

findings.summary: Runtime dependency seams are reimplemented as duplicated function-bag structs across subsystems, creating high indirection with low reuse.
evidence: Each module defines large runtime structs of function fields, then separately defines default constructors and manual nil-check merge logic.
RectifyRuntime and StartupRuntime both use repeated per-field merge functions, and orchestration repeats the same pattern for multiple Runtime*Ops groups plus dockerRuntime sub-ops.
The same seam concept is restated rather than shared, so adding/changing one dependency requires parallel edits in multiple runtimes and tests.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared seam pattern (e.g., small capability interfaces + common merge helper or builder) and split runtime bags by use-case boundaries so each subsystem depends only on the minimal capability set it consumes.

findings.summary: Adapter layers copy runtime fields without adding behavior, resulting in wrapper chains that increase call depth but not abstraction value.
evidence: wireguardRuntimeFromDocker and urbitRuntimeFromDocker are mostly direct field-copy translators from dockerRuntime sub-ops into another function-bag type.
defaultRuntimeLoad().LoadWireguard and defaultStartupLoad().LoadWireguard invoke wireguardRuntimeFromDocker(newDockerRuntime()).LoadWireguard, adding an extra assembly layer without policy checks.

Network access is repeatedly wrapped via inline closures creating new NetworkRuntime instances (e.g., AddOrGetNetwork and GetContainerNetwork paths), which hides concrete dependencies behind thin pass-through functions.
findings.suggestion: Collapse pure pass-through adapters by exposing focused typed capabilities directly (e.g., explicit WireguardLoader/NetworkOps dependencies) and keep wrappers only where they enforce policy, caching, validation, or translation.

findings.summary: Broad config models leak across orchestration and runtime boundaries, coupling unrelated domains through shared mega-structures.
evidence: SysConfig and UrbitDocker embed many concerns (runtime, network, schedule, web, feature, backup), and many modules consume snapshots or mutators against this shared wide model.
config_update_schema uses reflective patch application over many cross-domain fields, indicating a single broad mutation surface rather than bounded domain APIs.
Collectors/startup/main read config snapshots directly for many responsibilities, so changes in config structure propagate across broadcast, startup, and orchestration layers.
findings.suggestion: Define narrower domain configuration interfaces/DTOs (network, runtime, backup, feature) and route each subsystem through those contracts; keep SysConfig as persistence shape only and map it at boundaries.

findings.summary: Transition command wrappers in rectify add extra abstraction layers that mostly delegate, diluting interface honesty.
evidence: rectify defines multiple command structs implementing Apply that immediately forward to service/applier methods (e.g., urbitTransitionCommand, startramTransitionCommand, startramRetrieveTransition).
broadcast already provides a transition interface and mutation helpers, so many wrapper command types serve as indirection glue rather than domain abstractions.
The pattern repeats per transition type, increasing type count and routing complexity for small behavior differences.
findings.suggestion: Replace delegating command structs with direct reducer/service registrations or a table-driven dispatcher using typed handlers; keep dedicated command types only where they encapsulate distinct policy/state.

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime assembly is repeated in parallel structures, creating drift-prone design duplication.
evidence: `runtime_ops.go` defines default ops bundles (`defaultRuntimeContainerOps`, `defaultRuntimeUrbit`, etc.) with explicit function wiring.
`runtime_docker.go` builds another large function-wired runtime (`newDockerRuntime`) that remaps many of the same capabilities into different nested structs.
`runtime_core.go` then remaps again into specialized runtimes (`NewDockerTransitionRuntime`, `NewDockerHealthRuntime`).
`rectify.go` repeats a similar per-field merge/default pattern (`mergeRectify*` + `defaultRectifyRuntime`), showing the same manual composition pattern outside orchestration.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical dependency registry per subsystem and generate narrowed views through typed adapters/composition helpers instead of hand-copying field mappings in each runtime constructor.

findings.summary: System package files aggregate unrelated policies and platform operations in single modules.
evidence: `system.go` contains hardware telemetry (`GetMemory`, `GetCPU`, `GetTemp`), disk label/mount traversal (`GetDisk`), and cron/script lifecycle (`FixerScript`, `addCron`).
`disk.go` combines block device inspection, SMART policy checks, filesystem formatting/fstab reconciliation, and `/tmp` relocation logic (`SetupTmpDir`).
`wifi.go` includes runtime wiring, radio/device discovery, connection control, and global state loop orchestration in one package-level stateful module.
findings.suggestion: Split `system` into focused components (telemetry, storage provisioning, tmp policy, wifi control) with narrow interfaces, then keep orchestration logic in a coordinator layer rather than in utility-heavy files.

findings.summary: Config updates rely on reflective string-key dispatch plus separate JSON map persistence, which obscures invariants and ownership.
evidence: `config_update_schema.go` stores patch metadata as string keys/field names/apply paths and mutates via reflection (`confPatchFieldValue`, `confPatchSetConfigValue`) with panic paths for mismatches.
`persistence.go` converts typed config structs to generic maps via marshal/unmarshal (`structToMap`) before writing, creating a second mutation representation with different failure modes.
Large config tests in `config_test.go` validate many merge edge-cases, signaling high behavioral surface area that currently depends on implicit mapping conventions rather than explicit typed APIs.
findings.suggestion: Replace reflective apply paths with explicit typed mutators per config section and consolidate persistence/update flow around one typed model with compile-time field linkage.

findings.summary: Test suites repeatedly hand-roll global seam reset and fixture wiring patterns instead of using shared test infrastructure.
evidence: `disk_test.go` defines a long `resetDiskSeams` that snapshots/restores many package-level function vars.
`backupsvc/service_test.go` duplicates the same dependency-reset style via `resetBackupsvcDependencies`.
`importer/importer_test.go` has custom reset/override helpers for upload sessions and auth token seams.
`wifi_test.go` adds additional local runtime helper setup (`testWifiRuntime`) and empty reset placeholder (`resetWifiSeamsForTest`), indicating inconsistent conventions.
findings.suggestion: Adopt a shared test seam harness (common helper package) for snapshot/restore and fixture builders so stateful package-level overrides are centralized and consistent.

findings.summary: Urbit operation runtime file centralizes many distinct domain transitions with repeated template blocks and embedded side-effect orchestration.
evidence: `urbit_operations_runtime.go` contains numerous transition handlers (`togglePower`, `toggleDevMode`, `toggleNetwork`, domain setters) that each mix transition signaling, config mutation, and docker/click side effects.
Many handlers repeat near-identical transition template scaffolding (`runUrbitTransitionTemplateFn` setup with similar start/clear/error behavior), suggesting extraction opportunities.

`leak/handler.go` and orchestration runtime files show adjacent concerns also handling transport/state transitions, but there is no clear shared boundary for operation orchestration vs transport/event adaptation.
findings.suggestion: Refactor Urbit operations into smaller command handlers grouped by concern (power/network/domain/backups) and extract a reusable transition execution policy object to remove repeated template setup.

findings.summary: Importer workflow helper always returns an error, even when the underlying workflow succeeds.
evidence: goseg/importer/importer.go:927 returns fmt.Errorf("run imported pier workflow for %s: %w", ctx.Patp, runtime.runImportedPierWorkflowFn(ctx, runtime)) without checking nil first.
Because %w receives nil, callers still get a non-nil wrapped error string, causing success paths to be treated as failures.
Existing importer tests focus on configureUploadedPierForRuntime and do not directly guard runImportedPierWorkflowForRuntime's nil-success contract.
findings.suggestion: Assign runtime.runImportedPierWorkflowFn(...) to a local err variable, return nil when err == nil, and only wrap when err != nil; add a focused test for nil-success behavior in runImportedPierWorkflowForRuntime.

findings.summary: Rectify transition paths suppress config persistence errors, returning success despite partial state-commit failure.
evidence: goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go:64-66 logs UpdateConfTypedFn failure and continues.
goseg/rectify/rectify_services.go:129-152 logs warnings for UpdateUrbitWebConfigFn/UpdateUrbitNetworkConfigFn failures and still completes with return nil at line 157.
goseg/config/persistence.go consistently treats persistence failures as returned errors, creating inconsistent failure semantics across state mutation boundaries.
findings.suggestion: Define explicit best-effort vs required persistence policy for Rectify: either propagate these errors (possibly aggregated with errors.Join) or return a typed partial-failure error so callers can react instead of assuming success.

findings.summary: Upload auth/session failures rely on ad-hoc error strings, producing brittle and inconsistent handling contracts.
evidence: goseg/importer/importer.go returns plain string errors for key auth/session paths (e.g., "token mismatch", "upload token id is not authorized", "invalid upload session").
goseg/importer/importer_test.go validates these via string matching (e.g., lines 308-310 and 436-438), coupling behavior to wording.
The same module already uses sentinel/wrapped errors elsewhere (e.g., errImportPierConfig in finalize flow), making error strategy inconsistent within one subsystem.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed/sentinel errors for upload auth/session categories and wrap with context (`fmt.Errorf("...: %w", Err...)`); update tests and callers to use `errors.Is`/`errors.As` instead of message substring checks.

findings.summary: Cron existence check converts all probe failures into 'not found', unlike neighboring code that distinguishes benign vs fatal errors.
evidence: goseg/system/system.go:208-212 returns false for any `crontab -l` error, including permission/runtime/tooling failures.
goseg/system/system.go:223-227 in addCron explicitly differentiates 'no crontab for' from real errors and returns non-benign failures.
goseg/system/system_test.go currently has no coverage for this cron error classification path, so the inconsistency can regress unnoticed.
findings.suggestion: Make cronExists return `(bool, error)` and classify only the known benign 'no crontab' case as `(false, nil)`; propagate other errors to FixerScript so startup behavior is predictable and diagnosable.
